cataloging_subject.label: The Assembly of Gods
cataloging_subject.scope_note: The anonymous late-fifteenth-century Middle English dream-vision allegory conventionally called The Assembly of Gods, together with the short transmitted prose table interpreting twenty-seven mythological names. The record distinguishes this intellectual work from John Lydgate's different poem Reason and Sensuality, the Trinity and British Library witnesses, the early printed editions, Oscar Lovell Triggs's 1895 scholarly edition, Jane Chance's 1999 edition, the University of Michigan OCR/TEI representation, and the repository XML artifact.

catalog_summary.title: The Assembly of Gods
catalog_summary.author: Anonymous
catalog_summary.date: Probably c. 1478–1483; first known in Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19 and printed by about 1494

notes: An established editorial title rather than an authorial title; the early witnesses and prints use several different forms.

titles.value: The Assembly of Gods: Or, The Accord of Reason and Sensuality in the Fear of Death

scope.description: Title used in early Wynkyn de Worde printings

titles.value: Banquet of Gods and Goddesses, with the Discourse of Reason and Sensuality
notes: A historically attested cataloging title retained in Chance's edition title.

titles.value: The Discord betwixt Reason and Sensualytie

scope.description: Seventeenth-century table-of-contents title in Royal MS 18 D II

titles.value: Whan Phebus in the Crabbe had nere hys cours ronne

agents.name: John Lydgate
notes: c. 1370–1449/1450; retained only as the historically attributed author.

agents.name: Oscar Lovell Triggs
notes: 1865–1930; editor of the 1895 University of Chicago edition and 1896 EETS issue.

agents.name: Jane Chance
notes: Editor of the 1999 Middle English Texts Series edition used here as current scholarly interpretation and comparison.

assertion.notes: Modern scholarship and the current British Library component description treat the poem as anonymous.
The history of the attribution is confirmed; Lydgate's authorship is not. Chance summarizes the metrical, stylistic, and chronological reasons for rejection.

notes: Triggs defended the attribution and the U-M bibliographic record inherits it, but the cataloged work should not be filed as accepted Lydgate authorship.

scope.description: Text based principally on Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19
Variants from Royal MS 18 D II and selected early-print readings
Later 1999 comparison edition, not the source of the U-M encoding

assertion.notes: Chance follows Bradford Y. Fletcher's dating and localization of the Trinity copy context; neither source names an authorial date.

notes: This late date is a principal reason the poem cannot be by Lydgate, who died decades earlier.

date_statements.display: Trinity College Cambridge MS R.3.19, conventionally c. 1480 within a manuscript produced c. 1478–1483

assertion.notes: The current Trinity catalog gives only fifteenth/sixteenth centuries for the composite volume; the narrower range is scholarly.

date_statements.display: By about 1494, in Wynkyn de Worde's STC 17007 edition according to the current ISTC dating

assertion.notes: Current CERL/ISTC dates STC 17007 about 1494, while Triggs and Chance retain an older sequence in which Text C (STC 17005, 1498) precedes Text D (STC 17007, then dated c. 1500).

notes: No exact normalized year is supplied because the imprint is undated and the sequence remains bibliographically contested.

assertion.notes: Chance calls it a London poem on the basis of Fletcher's manuscript study; the text itself does not name its place of composition.

language_statements.label: Middle English
notes: The U-M header's generic code eng describes the scanned volume; the cataloged medieval poem is Middle English.

form_statements.description: The substantive work is 301 seven-line rhyme-royal stanzas; the brief twenty-seven-entry prose interpretatio is modeled as a separate framing part and does not make the whole work mixed.
A short prose list glossing twenty-seven divine or mythological names.

subject_statements.label: Conflict and accord between reason and sensuality
Classical mythology interpreted through Christian moral theology
Free will, conscience, sacraments, and salvation

scope.description: Vice's captains and their social catalogue, especially lines 617–714

summaries.value: A troubled dreamer who cannot reconcile Reason and Sensuality is led by Morpheus into a vast allegorical vision. A dispute among pagan gods develops into a psychomachia in which Virtue and Vice contend for the microcosm under Free Will and Conscience; Doctrine finally interprets the spectacle through Christian moral teaching, mortality, sacrament, and salvation.

The transmitted unit begins with a prose interpretation of twenty-seven mythological names, followed by 2,107 verse lines in 301 rhyme-royal stanzas. Chance analyzes the poem as an opening dream frame (lines 1–35), the assembly proper (36–616), the battle of Virtue and Vice (617–1468), and the concluding doctrinal dream frame (1469–2107).

The poem is not Lydgate's distinct Reason and Sensuality. Triggs's historical attribution and c. 1420 date are retained as reception history, while anonymous authorship and the later c. 1478–1483 dating are preferred.

extent.description: Repository XML metrics: one twenty-seven-entry prose interpretatio and 301 numbered LG stanza groups containing exactly 2,107 L elements. The five sections are the cataloger's analytical parts below; the XML itself encodes the poem as one DIV1.

content_parts.label: The Interpretation of the Names of Gods and Goddesses
content_parts.summary: A prose list glossing twenty-seven mythological figures from Phebus through Attropos.
notes: Royal MS 18 D II omits this list; Triggs reports that early print Text C includes it.

content_parts.label: Opening dream frame
content_parts.summary: The narrator's failed attempt to reconcile Reason and Sensuality, his falling asleep, and Morpheus's summons.

content_parts.label: Assembly proper
content_parts.summary: The jurisdictional dispute among Eolus, Diana, and Neptunus and the banquet of the gods.

content_parts.label: Psychomachia of Virtue and Vice
content_parts.summary: Vice and the deadly sins oppose Virtue, the virtues, liberal arts, Reason, Conscience, and Free Will in the microcosm.
notes: The analytical endpoint falls within a stanza, so no whole-stanza count is asserted.

content_parts.label: Concluding dream frame and doctrinal interpretation
content_parts.summary: The dreamer's encounter with Wit and Dame Doctrine, the interpretation of the allegory, awakening, and closing exhortation.
notes: The analytical starting point falls within a stanza, so no whole-stanza count is asserted.

related_works.title: Epistle of Othea
notes: Chance identifies significant influence from Christine de Pizan's Epistre Othea, probably through Stephen Scrope's Middle English translation; this is not modeled as a proven direct adaptation edge.

related_works.title: Reason and Sensuality
notes: A distinct poem by John Lydgate; similar catalog titles must not conflate it with The Assembly of Gods.

evidence.locator: HEADER; BODY interpretation-of-names and poem divisions; BACK milestones
evidence.summary: Identifies ACW2946.0001.001, U-M's 2006 uncorrected-OCR production statement, Triggs's 1895 source edition, the inherited Lydgate attribution, twenty-seven-name interpretatio, complete 301-stanza poem, and page-image milestones.

evidence.citation: XPath and element-count inspection of repository acw2946.xml performed 2026-07-11.
evidence.summary: Counts 27 ITEM entries in the prose interpretatio, 301 LG stanza groups, 2,107 L elements, 196 PB milestones, and six DIV1 containers, of which four are omitted front/back matter or dedication.

evidence.citation: University of Michigan Library Digital Collections, Corpus of Middle English Prose and Verse item ACW2946.0001.001.
evidence.summary: Confirms the item title, Triggs's 1895 Chicago edition, inherited Lydgate heading, public access, U-M's current public-domain statement, and No Copyright–United States rights label.

evidence.citation: Oscar Lovell Triggs, ed., The Assembly of Gods: Or, The Accord of Reason and Sensuality in the Fear of Death (Chicago: University of Chicago Press, 1895), complete Internet Archive scan assemblyofgodsor00lydgrich.
evidence.locator: Title page; preface; introduction pp. vii–xiv; text pp. 1–61; notes and glossary pp. 62–116
evidence.summary: Establishes the immediate edition's bibliography and practices: Trinity R.3.19 as authoritative copytext, Royal 18 D II collation, selected early-print readings, explicit emendations, editorial punctuation and proper-name capitalization, historical title forms, and Triggs's now-rejected attribution and dating arguments.

evidence.citation: Jane Chance, ed., The Assembly of Gods: Le Assemble de Dyeus, or Banquet of Gods and Goddesses, with the Discourse of Reason and Sensuality (Kalamazoo: Medieval Institute Publications, 1999), Middle English Texts Series digital edition.
evidence.locator: Introduction; manuscript and early-print bibliography; lines 1–2107; textual and explanatory notes
evidence.summary: Supplies current anonymous attribution, c. 1478–1483 London dating, 2,107-line/301-stanza extent, genres and subjects, Trinity copytext and folios, Royal and print witness list, critique of Triggs's transcription, and the competing Ringler/Fletcher stemmata.

evidence.citation: Trinity College Cambridge, James Catalogue of Western Manuscripts, MS R.3.19.
evidence.locator: Main details; contents at fols. 67v–97v; IIIF manifest
evidence.summary: Confirms current shelfmark, paper support, 255-folio extent, size, language, provenance, full public IIIF coverage, interpretatio at fol. 67v, poem at fols. 68r–97v, and the catalog page's CC BY-NC 4.0 footer.

evidence.citation: British Library Archives and Manuscripts Catalogue, Royal MS 18 D II and component ff. 167r–180v, Le assemble de dyeus.
evidence.locator: Component scope and content; parent codex description
evidence.summary: Identifies the anonymous Assembly component at fols. 167r–180v, its Lydgate attribution history and incipit; the parent record supplies later-phase dating c. 1516–1527, parchment, codex extent, dimensions, contents, access restrictions, and the current unavailability of former digital images.

evidence.citation: Consortium of European Research Libraries, Incunabula Short Title Catalogue records il00404000, il00404100, and il00405000.
evidence.locator: Imprints, holdings, references, notes, and digital-reproduction fields
evidence.summary: Provides current dates and holdings for STC 17005 (1498), STC 17006 (about 1499–1500), and STC 17007 (about 1494), including exact BL and Cambridge shelfmarks and Jenkinson's 1906 facsimile reference.

evidence.citation: General Corpus lineage manifest for acw2946.
evidence.summary: Binds the work record to Triggs's edition, Trinity and Royal witnesses, early prints, U-M encoding and page images, complete Trinity IIIF facsimile, later Chance edition, and the repository artifact.

open_questions.question: Can the anonymous Assembly-poet be identified more precisely, or the c. 1478–1483 London localization independently refined?
next_steps: Consult scholarship subsequent to Chance and Fletcher on the Hammond scribe, Trinity anthology booklets, dialect, paper stocks, and literary networks.
Do not revive the Lydgate attribution merely from early witness headings or U-M catalog inheritance.

open_questions.question: What is the exact descent among Trinity R.3.19, Royal 18 D II, and the early Wynkyn de Worde editions?
next_steps: Collate the complete Trinity and early-print facsimiles against Royal 18 D II when its images return.
Reconcile Ringler's B-from-D and C-first argument, Fletcher's competing common-ancestor model, and current ISTC's about-1494 dating of STC 17007.

open_questions.question: What is the current exact Bodleian shelfmark and access route for the Douce binding fragment preserving lines 1213–1323?
next_steps: Consult Ringler's 1953 article and ask Bodleian Special Collections to resolve the obsolete fragment description and former STC 24844a designation.
Add a witness entity only after the exact physical object and current shelfmark are verified.

notes: The Trinity prose interpretatio is retained as a framing part because it travels with the poem in the authoritative manuscript and several early prints; it is not counted among the poem's 2,107 verse lines.

The source XML's 196 PB milestones cover the complete 1895 volume, but only the title page, dedication, interpretatio, and poem are transcribed; most of Triggs's introduction and back matter are represented as omitted-matter containers with page milestones.
All manuscript dates, rights, and image-access facts remain attached to their material or digital layers rather than being promoted to properties of the abstract work.
